content: 1. Introduction The operation of electromembrane systems (EMS) in overlimit current modes has significant potential for a significant reduction in investment costs for desalination plants. The study by Wessling et al. [1] was devoted to the analysis of electroconvective vortices using the surface patterning method or geometric and electrical heterogeneity. In [1], a comparison of electroconvection development on two membranes modified using patterns was performed. An analysis was carried out of the increase in area of the electroconvective vortices, their structural stability in a steady state, and the possibility of controlling electroconvective vortices by introducing certain structural heterogeneities of the membrane surface to reduce energy losses in the current–voltage characteristic (CVC) and for more economical operation in EMS in overlimit modes, significantly increasing their efficiency.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: As [2–7] show, the phenomenon of overlimit mass transfer is currently being intensively studied; however, many issues are still poorly understood. For example, it is necessary to study the effects modifications of the membrane surface have on the development of electroconvection. Electrical inhomogeneity arises due to the inhomogeneous distribution of charges on the membrane surface, which can be caused by both the geometric features of the system [8,9] and the properties of the membrane material. Such inhomogeneities lead to local zones with increased electric field strength, which in turn affects the rate of ion migration and accordingly the emergence and development of electroconvective vortices. In turn, this leads to an increase in the rate of ion transfer through the membrane.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: In [10], it was shown that the electrical conductivity from an electrolyte solution to a solid element with a selective charge, such as an ion exchange membrane or an electrode, becomes unstable when the electrolyte concentration near the interface approaches zero due to diffusion limitation. In [11], an analysis was made of the influence of the fine structure of the electrical double layer and the reaction rate of the electrode on the choice of the wavelength of the increasing perturbation mode with morphological instability during cathodic electrodeposition from a dilute electrolyte solution. In recent years, there has been significant progress in understanding the role of electrical heterogeneity and electroconvection in EMS. Thus, a previous study [12] was devoted to the mechanism of development of electroconvective vortices, and their development was also controlled using a one-dimensional ion exchange membrane with a mesh pattern (IEM). It was shown that the rate of decrease in the electric potential rapidly increased during the development of vortices but gradually decreased during the transverse merging of vortices. Based on these data on the electroconvective vortex, it was shown that the electrical inhomogeneity of the IEM actively controls the vortex and increases the desalination efficiency.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: A new model was proposed in [13] that takes into account non-linear effects in electroconvection, which allowed for a more accurate description of mass transfer processes under high current density conditions. In [14], non-stationary ion transport caused by a sinusoidal current through an ion exchange membrane with an electrically inhomogeneous surface was considered. Low frequency spectra of the electrochemical impedance of electrically inhomogeneous ion exchange membranes were studied experimentally and theoretically, and a two-dimensional model for calculating these spectra was proposed. Non-stationary ion transport through a heterogeneous membrane and two adjacent diffusion layers was studied. Numerical and analytical (under conditions of equality of the diffusion coefficients of the cation and anion in the solution and at zero direct bias current) solutions were obtained for the corresponding two-dimensional boundary value problem. In [15], the study focused on experiments with heterogeneous membranes MK-40 and MA-41 with different ion exchange resin contents. It was found that the electrical conductivity of both cation exchange and anion exchange membranes increased with the increasing ion exchange resin content, with cation exchange membranes demonstrating a wider range of change. Thus, the study of electrical heterogeneity and its effect on ion transport with electroconvection taken into account is an important area of research into increasing the efficiency of the separation and purification of solutions, and is of great practical importance. Further theoretical research in this area should be aimed at developing more accurate mathematical models.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: The advantage of constructing 3D models with axial symmetry is the property of equal accessibility at sub-limiting currents, i.e., the fact that the thickness of the diffusion layer in such systems is constant up until the moment of the onset of electroconvection. At the same time, we note the insufficient development of such mathematical models and numerical methods for solving boundary value problems for overlimit current modes. This article is a continuation of [16], where the basic 3D model of electroconvection with axial symmetry was formulated and investigated. In this article, a modification of the basic model is considered, which consists of considering a cation exchange membrane of a more general type with conductivity and non-conductivity sections. As a result, an annular cation exchange membrane disk is obtained, new boundary conditions are formulated for non-conductivity sections, and their coordination with the conductivity sections on the cation exchange membrane is achieved. Thus, a mathematical model of a much more general type is obtained, which accordingly is more adequate and applicable for the study of heterogeneous cation exchange membranes used in practice than that considered in [16].

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: 2. Methods 2.1. Mathematical Model of Transport for Annular Membrane Disk Let us consider the transfer of salt ions during rotation of a cation exchange membrane disk, with non-conducting and conducting regions, inside a vertically standing cylindrical cell around the central axis, taking into account electroconvection, after which we will formulate the corresponding mathematical model. When formulating a mathematical model, as well as the subsequent numerical solution due to axial symmetry, it is sufficient to describe half of the cross-section of the cylindrical region, where it is necessary to determine the equations and boundary conditions. To do this, it is necessary to move from a rectangular coordinate system (x, y, z) to a cylindrical one (r, φ, z). However, the velocities in the angular direction differ from zero, so the model must include all three components of velocity (radial, azimuthal and axial), the flows, and the electric field strength, although they depend only on two arguments (r, z), i.e., a 2D model. Moreover, with axial symmetry, the derivatives with respect to the component φ are zero, so all equations in the cylindrical coordinate system can be simplified in this case.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: To model the transfer processes, a coupled system of Navier–Stokes equations with volumetric electric force f and Nernst–Planck–Poisson equations was used, which in a cylindrical coordinate system (r, φ, z) takes the form given in [16]. Here, u = (u, v, w) are radial, azimuthal, and axial components of the solution flow velocity; index 1 refers to cations, index 2 to anions; j1, j2 are flows; C1, C2 are concentrations; z1, z2 are charge numbers; I is current density; D1, D2 are diffusion coefficients; Φ is electric field potential; E = -∇Φ is electric field strength; ε is dielectric permeability of the electrolyte; F is Faraday constant; R is universal gas constant; T is absolute temperature; t is time; ρ is density; η is dynamic viscosity; p is pressure.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: In the studied section of the cylindrical region, boundary 2 models the part of space infinitely distant from the CEM, in which the condition of electroneutrality is satisfied and the concentration of the solution is constant (C0). Boundary 2 is also considered an anode and an open boundary (inlet) for the solution; for the velocity here the condition of the absence of normal voltage is set, and the pressure is considered equal to zero. This boundary is considered an equipotential surface, and Φ(t, r, 0) = dφ. Boundary 1 is the axis of symmetry. Boundary 5 is considered the outlet for the solution; therefore, the condition of removal of ions only by convective flow is set as j_i = -u·C_i, i=1,2, and for the velocity the condition is the absence of normal stress. In addition, for the potential, the boundary condition -n·(r ∂Φ/∂r, ∂Φ/∂z)^T = 0 is used.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: Boundaries 3 and 4 correspond to the rotating CEM, while for the azimuthal velocity the condition is v(t, r, H) = ωr, where ω is the angular velocity of rotation of the disk. For anions, the condition of impermeability (absence of flow) is set as -n·j_2 = 0. The conductivity region is located at boundary 4, so the cation exchange membrane forms a ring. At boundary 3, corresponding to the non-conducting region, there is a condition of impermeability -n·j_i = 0, i=1,2. To derive the condition for the potential at the non-conducting boundary 3, the equality of the current density I to zero at this boundary is used. The normal component of the current density at this boundary is equal to zero due to the conditions on the flows. From the condition that the radial component of the current density is zero, we obtain the radial potential gradient condition. Thus, at boundary 3, the following condition must be set for the potential: ∂Φ/∂r = -RT (z1 D1 ∂C1/∂r + z2 D2 ∂C2/∂r) / (F (z1^2 D1 C1 + z2^2 D2 C2)). It is assumed that the cell is initially completely filled with an ideally mixed solution of sodium chloride with concentration C0, and the same ideally mixed solution is fed into it through boundary 2. Therefore, a constant concentration is taken as the initial condition C1(0, r, z) = C2(0, r, z) = C0.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: 2.2. Algorithm for Hybrid Numerical–Analytical Solution In order to clarify the problems of the numerical solution of the boundary value problem, we move to a dimensionless form using characteristic quantities. The problem has two characteristic quantities: H (cell height) and r0 (outer radius of the annular membrane). Since in practice H = a·r0 with a=1÷5, they have the same order, and r0 is taken as a characteristic quantity. As the characteristic velocity V0, we use the azimuthal component V0 = ω0 r0. Dimensionless parameters: Peclet number Pe = r0V0/D0 ≈ 0.75·10^3·ω0 (large, convective transport prevails); ε(u) = εRT/(r0^2 C0 F^2) ≈ 2.3·10^{-12}/C0 (small parameter, ratio of square of Debye length to square of r0); Reynolds number Re = r0V0/ν ≈ 0.994 ω0 (inertia and viscous friction comparable); Kel = RTC0/(ρ r0^2 ω0^2), and Kek = Kel·ε(u).

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: The presence of a small parameter ε(u) at the derivative in the Poisson equation shows that the problem belongs to the type of singularly perturbed problems for a quasilinear system of partial differential equations. As mentioned, a diffusion layer arises near the CEM, consisting of an electroneutrality region and a space charge region directly adjacent to the membrane. At overlimiting currents, an extended space charge region (SCR) appears. In the quasi-equilibrium SCR, the migration flow is equal to the diffusion flow in the first approximation, leading to the current becoming zero in the first approximation. Concentrations and electric field strength increase exponentially, causing very large gradients. This makes the boundary value problem stiff and difficult to solve numerically, requiring exponential grid changes and very small time steps, leading to error accumulation. A numerical study of the quasi-equilibrium SCR established its main properties: it forms almost instantly (10^{-5} s); its thickness does not depend on r (except near r=0); it is quasi-stationary; radial and axial velocities are close to zero, azimuthal velocity ≈ ωr.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: Thus, a hybrid numerical–analytical method is proposed: analytical solution in the quasi-equilibrium region (region of increasing cations, RICC) combined with numerical solution in the remaining region. At sub-limit currents, the RICC coincides with the quasi-equilibrium SCR; at overlimit currents, it almost coincides. The boundary between RICC and the rest of the solution is the point where cation concentration has a local minimum. In the RICC, the system reduces to 1D equations in z (due to independence from r and time). For the sub-limit case (pre-limit), after substitution ξ = (z-1)/√ε, the system reduces to Boltzmann–Debye equations, with an approximate analytical solution given for E(z,ε), C1, C2. For the overlimit case, anions are absent in (zm,1], leading to another approximate analytical solution for E(z,ε) and C1(z,ε). Splicing conditions determine the constants and the boundary zm. The algorithm involves: (1) numerical solution without RICC to find C1(1,ε), C2(1,ε); (2) calculate potential jump; (3) analytical solution in RICC; (4) combine to get full solution.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: 3. Results 3.1. Computational Experiments for Verification of Hybrid Numerical Method To verify the results, comparison with independent finite element method (FEM) was used. A grid of 9581 elements was sufficient; time step 0.01 s. At low concentrations (0.01, 0.1 mol/m^3 and below), FEM is preferable; the hybrid method works better with increasing initial concentrations (0.1, 1, 10 mol/m^3 and beyond). In the general area of applicability, the methods coincide with about 1% accuracy. Figures 2 and 3 show cation concentration and solution flow lines for annular disk model at C0 = 0.01 and 0.1 mol/m^3, comparing hybrid and FEM. Maximum concentration occurs near the boundary between non-conducting and conducting regions, and also on the symmetry axis. Vortices form in the conductivity region, and their number increases with concentration.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: 3.2. Basic Laws of Occurrence and Development of Electroconvection and Mass Transfer for the Model with an Annular Membrane Numerical experiments were conducted with C0 from 0.01 to 100 mol/m^3, angular velocity ω from π/2 to 4π rad/s, potential jump from 0.1 to 2 V. At small potential jumps (0.1 V, ω=π/2 rad/s, C0=10 mol/m^3), vortices do not appear; flow lines are logarithmic spirals. A diffusion layer forms with approximately constant thickness except near the junction of non-conductivity and conductivity regions. With higher potential jumps (0.3 V and 1.5 V) at C0=0.01 mol/m^3, electroconvection appears only when the potential jump exceeds a threshold that increases with angular velocity. For real concentrations (1–10 mol/m^3), at ω=π/2 rad/s and dφ=1.5 V, maximum concentration occurs at the junction of non-conductivity and conductivity regions. Relaxation of boundary/initial conditions occurs within 2 s. Vortices arise at the junction and flow downstream, gradually decreasing in size due to increasing azimuthal velocity v=ωr.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection

content: 4. Discussion This article proposed a new mathematical model of the influence of inhomogeneity areas on the transfer of salt ions in systems with axial symmetry, taking into account electroconvection, as a boundary value problem for a non-stationary coupled system of Nernst–Planck–Poisson and Navier–Stokes equations in a cylindrical coordinate system. An algorithm for a hybrid numerical–analytical solution was formulated, allowing the boundary value problem for real values of initial concentration, potential jump, and disk rotation speed to be solved. Using the hybrid method, the process of formation of electroconvective vortices in a potentiostatic mode was numerically studied with the addition of conductivity and non-conductivity regions to a cation exchange rotating membrane disk, including in the form of an annular disk. It has been established that the vortex formation process can be enhanced by adding non-conductive regions to the CEM; vortices are formed at the junction of non-conductive and conductive regions. With small potential jumps, vortices do not appear, and flow lines are logarithmic spirals. Relaxation occurs within 2 s. Vortices arise at the junction and flow downstream, gradually decreasing in size. The mathematical models can be used to optimize desalination in axially symmetric EMS, and the hybrid numerical–analytical method can be applied to other boundary problems of membrane electrochemistry.

citation.title: Mathematical Modeling of the Influence of Electrical Heterogeneity on the Processes of Salt Ion Transfer in Membrane Systems with Axial Symmetry Taking into Account Electroconvection
